title: Peer Review & Publication
subtitle: Academic Surgery — Submission Strategy, Peer Review Process, Open Access & Research Integrity
guidelines_version: ICMJE Recommendations 2024 · COPE Guidelines 2022 · WAME 2023 · Plan S 2021
textbook_refs: ICMJE. Recommendations for the Conduct, Reporting, Editing, and Publication of Scholarly Work in Medical Journals. 2024
COPE. Core Practices. Committee on Publication Ethics. 2022
Bohannon J. Who's Afraid of Peer Review? Science 2013 (predatory journal investigation)
Tennant JP et al. The academic, economic and societal impacts of Open Access. F1000Research 2016
Larivière V et al. The oligopoly of academic publishers in the digital era. PLOS ONE 2015
learning_objectives: Apply ICMJE authorship criteria to define legitimate authorship and identify ghost/gift authorship
Describe the peer review process from submission to decision and identify its limitations
Distinguish open, single-blind, and double-blind peer review and evaluate ethical implications
Select journals using impact factor, CiteScore, scope, and turnaround time
Identify predatory journals using recognised red flags and verification tools
Construct an effective covering letter and respond to reviewer comments professionally
Distinguish Gold, Green, and Diamond Open Access routes and calculate APC cost-benefit
Describe the retraction and correction process and identify post-publication integrity mechanisms
Evaluate research impact using citations, H-index, and altmetrics
Use preprint servers appropriately and understand their limitations

blocks.content: Authorship of a peer-reviewed publication confers both academic credit and ethical responsibility. The credit-responsibility dyad is fundamental: authors receive recognition in their academic record, CV, and grant applications, and in exchange they are accountable for the integrity of the reported work. The International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE) defines authorship precisely through four criteria, all of which must be satisfied simultaneously by each individual listed as an author. The growing complexity of large-scale, multi-disciplinary, and multi-institutional research has made authorship criteria harder to apply but no less important.

ICMJE authorship requires ALL of the following four criteria: (1) Substantial contributions to conception/design OR acquisition/analysis/interpretation of data; (2) Drafting or critically revising the manuscript for important intellectual content; (3) Final approval of the version to be published; (4) Accountability for all aspects of the work — agreeing to investigate and resolve questions related to accuracy or integrity. Authorship confers credit AND responsibility. It is not earned by: funding the study, providing laboratory space, being head of department, or collecting data without contributing to analysis or writing.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Ghost and Gift Authorship
blocks.content: **Ghost authorship**: a person who made substantial intellectual contributions to the work is omitted from the author list — common in industry-sponsored trials where company statisticians do the analysis but do not appear as authors. **Gift authorship**: a person is listed as author who did not meet ICMJE criteria, typically a department head or senior colleague who contributed nothing to the specific study. Both practices violate ICMJE guidelines and are sanctionable by journals. The **Contributor Roles Taxonomy (CRediT)** provides a standardised vocabulary for specifying each author's actual contribution — increasingly required by high-impact journals.

blocks.title: CRediT Taxonomy — 14 Contributor Roles
rows: Ideas; formulation of overarching research goals and aims
Identifying the clinical question and hypothesis
Design; creation of models and statistical analysis plans
Study design, sample size calculation, statistical plan
Application of statistical, mathematical, computational, or other analysis methods
Conducting the research process — patient enrolment, procedures
Lead author writing the first draft
Critical revision of the manuscript for intellectual content
Oversight and leadership of the research team
Senior author supervision — counts toward authorship only with other contributions
Grant acquisition — insufficient alone for authorship
Research coordination — insufficient alone for authorship

blocks.content: After submission, a manuscript undergoes editorial screening (scope, plagiarism check via iThenticate, basic quality) before being assigned to an editor who selects 2–3 peer reviewers. Peer review types: **single-blind** (reviewers know authors; authors don't know reviewers — most common), **double-blind** (both anonymous), **open peer review** (identities known; increasingly used by BMJ, PLOS Medicine). Reviewer decisions: accept, minor revision, major revision, reject with resubmit, reject. **Major revision** is the most common response for publishable work — treat it as a positive outcome. The average time from submission to first decision at a top surgical journal is 4–8 weeks; total time from submission to publication is 6–18 months.

blocks.title: Peer Review Types — Comparison
rows: Reviewers know authors; authors don't know reviewers
Reduces reviewer retaliation risk; most familiar to reviewers
Reviewer bias toward prestigious institutions; gender bias documented
Neither reviewers nor authors know each other's identity
Reduces prestige bias; protects junior authors
Difficult to achieve in small fields; identifiable by writing style
Both identities disclosed; reviews often published
Accountability; civility; transparency; reduces reviewer conflicts of interest
Reviewer reluctance; retaliation risk in competitive fields
Rapid error detection; ongoing quality monitoring

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Responding to Reviewers
blocks.content: The response-to-reviewers letter is as important as the manuscript revision itself. Structure: (1) thank the editor and reviewers; (2) address every comment, numbered to match the reviewer's numbering; (3) for each change: state what you changed, where (page/line number), and paste the revised text; (4) for disagreement: provide evidence-based counter-argument respectfully — do not simply comply with a reviewer request that would compromise scientific accuracy. A well-structured response letter dramatically increases acceptance rates at revision stage.

**Reviewer conflicts of interest** are a significant and underappreciated source of bias in peer review. A reviewer has a conflict of interest when they have a financial relationship with the authors or their institution, a personal relationship (positive or negative), are direct academic competitors for the same grant funding or priority claim, or have publicly taken a strong position on the paper's topic. Most journals ask editors to avoid assigning competitors as reviewers, but the system relies heavily on self-declaration by editors and reviewers. Authors may suggest reviewers (names considered but not guaranteed) and may exclude specific individuals (exclusions are generally honoured), providing some protection against targeted hostile review.

blocks.title: Key Surgical Journals — Impact Factor and Scope
rows: All surgery; high-impact RCTs; landmark trials
Top-tier oncology RCTs; phase III trials

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Predatory Journals
blocks.content: Predatory journals exploit the open-access author-pays model without providing legitimate peer review. Red flags: unsolicited email invitations; rapid acceptance (days to 1 week); no verifiable peer review process; journal not indexed in PubMed/MEDLINE, Scopus, or DOAJ; inflated/fabricated impact factor claims; similar names to legitimate journals. Verification tools: **DOAJ** (Directory of Open Access Journals) for legitimate OA journals; **ISSN Portal** to verify journal existence; **Scimago** for indexed journals. The Bohannon sting (Science 2013) revealed widespread acceptance of a fatally flawed paper by 157 journals — demonstrating the scale of the predatory journal problem.

**Predatory conferences** parallel predatory journals. These are conferences that exist primarily to generate revenue through registration fees and publication fees, without providing genuine scientific peer review, quality control of presentations, or substantive academic networking. Red flags for predatory conferences: unsolicited invitation emails with excessive flattery; invitations to multiple, geographically diverse conferences from the same organiser; no verifiable scientific committee; no sponsorship from established professional societies (ESSO, ESSO, ESMO, ASES); abstract acceptance within 24–48 hours; excessive registration fees with no financial support for early-career researchers. Presenting at predatory conferences wastes resources and provides no legitimate academic credit.

sections.title: Open Access Publishing — Gold, Green & Diamond Routes

blocks.content: Open Access (OA) refers to the principle that published research should be freely available to any reader, anywhere, without payment or institutional subscription barriers. OA has moved from an academic ideal to a funder and legislative mandate in many countries. The European Research Council (ERC), Horizon Europe, Wellcome Trust, NIH, and Spanish ISCIII all mandate OA publication for funded research. Three primary OA routes exist, each with different cost, speed, and sustainability implications.

blocks.title: Open Access Routes — Gold, Green, and Diamond
rows: Published version freely available immediately on journal website under CC licence; author pays APC
Subscription journal with per-article OA option; author pays APC to unlock individual article
Annals of Surgery, BJS, EJSO — most major journals
Author self-archives accepted manuscript in institutional or subject repository after embargo period
PubMed Central (PMC), Zenodo, DIGITAL.CSIC, institutional repository
Yes — immediate CC-BY deposit to repository
Published open access; no APC to authors; journal funded by institution, society, or subsidy
PLOS Biology (subsidised); many society journals; emerging model
Freely readable on journal website but no reuse rights; no OA licence — not true OA
Many journal websites during COVID emergency access
No — does not meet Plan S

blocks.title: Plan S — Mandatory Immediate Open Access
blocks.content: Plan S is a 2018 initiative by cOAlition S (a consortium of research funders including ERC, Wellcome, many national funders) requiring that all research funded by member funders be published in compliant OA venues by 2021, with no embargo period. Key Plan S requirements: (1) immediate OA (no embargo); (2) CC-BY licence (allows reuse, adaptation, and commercial use with attribution); (3) published in a fully OA journal, in a hybrid journal under a Transformative Agreement, or self-archived in a compliant repository. Spanish ISCIII joined cOAlition S in 2022. If you receive ISCIII funding, your publications must comply. Check compliance via the Journal Checker Tool (journalcheckertool.org).

**Article Processing Charges (APCs)** are the fees charged by journals for Gold or Hybrid OA publication. APCs vary enormously: MDPI journals charge ~€1,000–€2,500; Nature journals charge €9,500–€11,000; Elsevier hybrid charges ~€3,000–€5,000 per article. APCs represent a significant and growing financial burden, particularly for researchers in low- and middle-income countries or without institutional funding. **APC waivers** are available from many publishers on a case-by-case basis: geographic waivers (automatic for authors from World Bank low-income countries), hardship waivers (for unfunded research), and institutional deals (transformative agreements between publishers and institutions that bundle subscription and APC costs). Researchers should explore waiver eligibility before assuming APCs must be paid personally.

Preprints are complete manuscript drafts posted to publicly accessible servers before (or in parallel with) formal peer review and journal submission. Preprint servers provide immediate free dissemination of research findings, allowing the scientific community to access, critique, and build upon results without waiting 6–18 months for the traditional publication process. Preprint use has grown dramatically in biomedical research since 2013 (bioRxiv launch) and especially since 2019 (medRxiv launch and COVID-19 acceleration).

blocks.title: Major Preprint Servers for Surgical and Medical Research
rows: Clinical medicine, surgery, epidemiology, public health
Basic screening (no peer review); health/safety alerts for COVID-related
Most medical journals accept prior posting
Cite as preprint with DOI; note 'not peer reviewed'
Most biology and some medical journals
Social sciences, economics, law, some clinical
No formal screening — highest risk of predatory use
Cite with caution; lower quality filter
Preprint server; also provides in-review preprints for Springer/Nature journals
Basic check; links to journal review
Lower prestige; suitable for rapid dissemination

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Citing Preprints as Established Evidence
blocks.content: Preprints have NOT undergone peer review. Citing a preprint as established evidence — particularly in clinical decision-making or systematic reviews — carries significant risk: preprints may contain errors, methodological flaws, or conclusions that change substantially after peer review. During the COVID-19 pandemic, multiple preprints on treatments were widely cited before peer review revealed serious methodological problems (e.g., the hydroxychloroquine preprints). Best practice: when citing a preprint in a manuscript, always label it clearly as a preprint ('Smith et al., medRxiv 2024, preprint not peer reviewed') and check at submission whether the preprint has since been published in a peer-reviewed journal.

Most major surgical journals now permit submission of manuscripts that have been posted as preprints, provided the preprint is linked in the submission and the journal is informed. ICMJE member journals explicitly allow preprinting as long as the journal is notified. However, some journals (particularly high-impact general journals) have complex policies on preprints and press releases — always verify the specific journal's preprint policy in their Instructions to Authors before posting. After publication of the peer-reviewed paper, authors should update the preprint record with a link to the published version to prevent confusion between the preprint and the final article.

Scientific literature is a living record that must be correctable when errors are identified. Three primary mechanisms exist for post-publication record correction: errata/corrigenda (minor errors), expressions of concern (investigations in progress), and retractions (serious errors invalidating conclusions). The distinction between these mechanisms matters: each has a different implication for the scientific record and for the authors' standing.

blocks.title: Post-Publication Correction Mechanisms
rows: The journal made an error in typesetting, layout, or production not attributable to authors
Correction notice published; original article unchanged
Erratum linked to original article in databases
Authors made an error in the submitted content that does not invalidate conclusions (e.g., transposition of values, figure labelling)
Correction published; original article amended or annotated
Indexed alongside original; correction must be complete
Serious concerns about integrity raised (misconduct allegation, institutional investigation pending) but facts not yet established
Notice published; article remains but flagged; readers warned
Used when investigation incomplete; does not prejudge outcome
Conclusions are unreliable due to data fabrication, falsification, plagiarism, duplicate publication, or major unresolvable error
Retraction notice replaces or annotates original; article marked RETRACTED in databases
Authors must cooperate; uncooperative retraction possible; reasons must be stated

blocks.title: COPE Retraction Guidelines — Author Obligations
blocks.content: COPE (Committee on Publication Ethics) guidelines state that authors have an obligation to notify editors immediately when they discover significant errors in their published work. If an error does not invalidate conclusions, a corrigendum should be submitted. If conclusions are affected, authors should proactively request retraction. Journals may retract articles without author consent if authors refuse to cooperate after being notified of serious concerns. Retracted articles remain in PubMed and databases but are clearly marked as retracted — they should not be cited as evidence in subsequent publications. Citing a retracted paper without acknowledging its retraction status is an ethical violation.

The **Retraction Watch** database (retractionwatch.com) tracks retractions across biomedical journals and provides a searchable database of retracted papers. As of 2024, over 45,000 papers have been retracted and indexed. Reasons for retraction include: data fabrication or falsification (most severe — typically career-ending); image manipulation (increasingly detected by AI screening tools such as Proofig); plagiarism (text-matching software iThenticate detects verbatim copying); duplicate publication (same data submitted to multiple journals without disclosure); and honest errors that invalidate conclusions. The time from publication to retraction has decreased significantly as post-publication peer review platforms (PubPeer) and automated image integrity tools have improved.

Research impact metrics quantify how widely a publication or researcher's work has been engaged with, cited, and disseminated. These metrics are used in grant applications, academic promotion decisions, departmental resource allocation, and journal ranking. Understanding the strengths and limitations of each metric is essential for an academic surgeon — both for interpreting their own track record and for critically evaluating claims made about journal quality or colleague productivity.

blocks.title: Research Impact Metrics — Definition, Strengths, and Limitations
rows: Total number of times a paper is cited by other papers
Scopus, Web of Science, Google Scholar
Includes self-citations and negative citations; field-dependent
Average citations per paper published by a journal in the prior 2 years
Journal-level (not paper-level); manipulable; biased toward review articles
Author has published H papers each cited at least H times (e.g., H=20 means 20 papers each cited ≥20 times)
Scopus, Web of Science, Google Scholar
Disadvantages early-career researchers; field-dependent; does not credit negative results
Average citations per document over 4 years (broader than IF)
More stable than 2-year IF; includes more document types
Composite score of social media mentions, news coverage, policy citations, downloads
Measures real-world translation and public engagement
Does not correlate with citation impact; easily gamed; unstable over time
Number of times the full text is accessed
Does not indicate citation; includes automated bots

blocks.title: Board Pearl — H-index in Academic Promotion
blocks.content: The H-index is increasingly used in academic promotion and grant panel decisions. Context is essential: an H-index of 15 in surgical oncology is impressive for a PGY5 resident but modest for a 20-year full professor. Field-adjusted norms exist: surgery (H~10–15 for senior lecturer level); basic science (H~20–25 for equivalent level). The H-index is NOT a linear scale — moving from H=10 to H=11 requires one more paper with 11+ citations, but moving from H=30 to H=31 requires a paper cited 31+ times, which takes years. Google Scholar gives the highest H-index (includes book chapters, conference abstracts); Web of Science the lowest (most restrictive); Scopus intermediate. Always specify the database when reporting your H-index.

**Impact factor manipulation** is a recognised problem in journal publishing. Methods include: encouraging self-citation (editors asking authors to add references to their journal); publishing large numbers of review articles (which accumulate citations rapidly); forming citation cartels between journals; and 'coercive citation' (reviewers requesting that authors add references to their own work). The journal impact factor is calculated by Clarivate based on Web of Science data — a journal's IF can be suppressed by Clarivate if manipulation is detected. Researchers should be aware that submitting to a journal whose reviewers repeatedly request unjustified self-citations is itself an ethics concern.

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of peer review, publication ethics, open access, and research integrity with 20 clinical questions.

questions.stem: A surgical registrar contributed to the statistical analysis and manuscript drafting of a multicentre trial. The department head, who provided funding and institutional oversight but did not analyse data or write any section of the manuscript, insists on being listed as last author. Which statement correctly applies ICMJE authorship criteria?

options.A: The department head qualifies as author because funding acquisition meets ICMJE criterion 1
options.B: The department head does not qualify as author — institutional oversight and funding alone do not satisfy ICMJE criteria
options.C: The department head should be listed as corresponding author to reflect seniority
options.D: All members of the research group are entitled to authorship

explanation.correct: ICMJE requires ALL four criteria for authorship. Funding acquisition and institutional oversight satisfy none of the four criteria individually or collectively. Listing the department head as author would constitute gift authorship — a recognised COPE and ICMJE violation. Appropriate recognition: Acknowledgements section with 'We thank Prof X for providing funding and institutional support.'

wrong.A: Funding acquisition is explicitly listed by ICMJE as insufficient alone for authorship.
wrong.C: Corresponding authorship does not substitute for meeting authorship criteria — the corresponding author must also meet all four criteria.
wrong.D: Group membership does not confer authorship — ICMJE is explicit that authorship is based on individual contributions.

explanation.guideline: ICMJE Recommendations 2024 — Criteria 1–4 all required; contributions insufficient for authorship should be acknowledged
explanation.trial: Marušić A et al. (PLOS One 2011): survey of surgical authors — 62% reported gift authorship in their last published paper.
explanation.pearl: When in doubt about authorship: ask whether the individual could defend the published work to a journal editor if questioned about data integrity. If not — they are not an author.

questions.stem: An editor at BJS invites a leading expert to review a manuscript. The expert recognises the work as coming from a rival group competing for the same grant funding on identical research. According to COPE guidelines, what is the expert's obligation?

options.A: The expert may review the manuscript as long as the review is objective and scientifically rigorous
options.B: The expert must decline the review invitation and disclose the conflict of interest to the editor
options.C: The expert may review but must recuse from commenting on the significance of the work
options.D: Competing for the same grant is not a recognised conflict of interest in peer review

explanation.correct: COPE guidelines define reviewer conflicts of interest as any relationship — financial, personal, academic, or competitive — that could bias the review. Competing for the same grant funding to conduct identical research is a direct competitive conflict of interest. The expert has both an incentive to delay publication (disadvantaging the competitors) and an interest in obtaining detailed knowledge of unpublished methodology. The ethical obligation is clear: decline the review and disclose the conflict to the editor so an alternative reviewer without this conflict can be recruited.

wrong.A: Good intention does not eliminate the structural bias created by a direct competitive conflict — the appearance of bias undermines the integrity of the process regardless of the reviewer's objectivity.
wrong.C: Partial recusal does not address the fundamental conflict — the reviewer still has access to unpublished data from a competitor.
wrong.D: Competing for the same grant is explicitly recognised by COPE and most journals as a material conflict of interest requiring disclosure.

explanation.guideline: COPE Ethical Guidelines for Peer Reviewers 2017 — 'Reviewers should not agree to review a manuscript if they have...a competitive relationship with the authors'
explanation.trial: Schroter S et al. Relationships between manuscript characteristics and acceptance rates of journals. J R Soc Med 2004.
explanation.pearl: COPE lists the following as mandatory reviewer conflict of interest disclosures: financial relationships with authors; institutional relationships; personal relationships; being a direct competitor; having previously expressed strong public views on the topic.

questions.stem: A surgeon submits an identical manuscript reporting a single-centre CRS+HIPEC cohort simultaneously to EJSO and Annals of Surgical Oncology, reasoning that parallel submission speeds up publication. Which publication ethics violation has been committed?

options.A: No violation — simultaneous submission is permitted if both journals are informed
options.B: Simultaneous submission without disclosure violates ICMJE policy and is considered duplicate submission
options.C: Simultaneous submission is only prohibited for randomised trials, not cohort studies
options.D: The violation is minor — the manuscript has not yet been accepted by either journal

explanation.correct: ICMJE explicitly prohibits simultaneous submission of the same manuscript to more than one journal. The rationale: peer reviewers dedicate unrewarded time; if both journals accept, only one can publish — wasting the other journal's editorial resources and peer review effort. The violation occurs at submission, not acceptance. If discovered (editors communicate, or the paper is accepted by both), both journals may reject or retract, and the editor community may be notified. The correct process is sequential submission — if rejected (or desk rejected without review), the manuscript may be submitted to the next journal.

wrong.A: Simultaneous submission is not permitted even with disclosure — it is prohibited by ICMJE policy regardless of transparency.
wrong.C: ICMJE simultaneous submission prohibition applies to all manuscript types: RCTs, cohort studies, case series, reviews, and case reports.
wrong.D: The violation is committed at submission — the stage of acceptance is irrelevant to whether the ethical breach occurred.

explanation.guideline: ICMJE Recommendations 2024 — 'Authors should not submit a manuscript that is under consideration by another journal'
explanation.trial: COPE flowchart C2: Handling of submissions that are simultaneously submitted to more than one journal.
explanation.pearl: If rejected without review (desk rejection) within 24–48 hours, immediate resubmission to the next journal is acceptable — desk rejection frees the manuscript for resubmission without a waiting period.

questions.stem: A research group publishes a full 5-year CRS+HIPEC outcomes paper in EJSO. Six months later, they submit a second paper to Annals of Surgical Oncology presenting the same dataset but restricted to patients with appendiceal pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP), without disclosing the parent publication. What publication ethics issue does this represent?

options.A: Duplicate publication — the same conclusions are being re-reported
options.B: Salami publishing — the dataset has been fragmented into minimally publishable units without justification or disclosure
options.C: No ethical issue — subgroup analyses of specific diagnoses constitute independent scientific contributions
options.D: Ghost authorship — if the same statistician analysed both datasets without being listed

explanation.correct: Salami publishing (data fragmentation) involves dividing a dataset that could be published as a single comprehensive paper into multiple smaller papers to maximise publication count from one body of work. The critical element here is non-disclosure of the parent publication — readers of the PMP paper would not know that this analysis comes from a larger dataset already published. This concealment is the ethical violation. Legitimate subgroup publications are acceptable when: the parent publication overlap is declared; the subgroup hypothesis is clinically and statistically pre-specified; and the subgroup analysis provides genuine additional information not available from the parent paper. Without disclosure, the PMP paper implies an independent cohort that does not exist.

wrong.A: Duplicate publication involves republishing the same analysis and conclusions — here, the subgroup is different, making this salami publishing rather than pure duplication.
wrong.C: Subgroup analyses are legitimate only with disclosure of the parent publication — non-disclosure makes this an ethical violation.
wrong.D: Ghost authorship involves concealing individual contributors — this is a distinct issue from data fragmentation.

explanation.guideline: COPE — 'Redundant publication and data fragmentation are unethical; all prior related publications using the same data must be declared at submission'
explanation.trial: Baggs JG et al. Duplicate publication and related issues. J Am Geriatr Soc 2008.
explanation.pearl: When submitting any paper that overlaps with a previously published dataset: declare it in the cover letter, the Methods section (with citation), and the conflict of interest statement. Proactively declaring overlap demonstrates good faith — concealing it constitutes misconduct.

questions.stem: A journal's editor-in-chief receives a request from a large pharmaceutical company to purchase 5,000 reprints of a clinical trial they sponsored, at €20 per reprint, totalling €100,000. The trial showed significant benefit for the company's drug. According to COPE guidelines, what is the primary concern?

options.A: Reprint sales are a legitimate and ethically unrestricted revenue stream for journals
options.B: Large reprint orders from industry sponsors of positive trials create a financial incentive for journals to accept industry-sponsored studies with positive results — a form of publication bias
options.C: The concern is copyright — the company requires a licence to reproduce the journal's content
options.D: The only issue is whether the trial's methodology is sound — publication should be based on science, not commercial factors

explanation.correct: Reprint orders from pharmaceutical companies have been identified as a major source of perverse financial incentives in journal publishing. A journal that accepts a trial paper knows that if the results are positive, the sponsor will purchase high-volume reprints — generating €50,000–€200,000 in revenue for a single paper. This creates a structural incentive to accept industry-sponsored positive trials over negative trials or independent studies, contributing to publication bias in the medical literature. COPE and ICMJE have called for transparency about reprint revenue. Some journals (BMJ, PLOS Medicine) have eliminated or limited reprint sales precisely to remove this incentive.

wrong.A: Reprint sales are legally permitted but are not ethically unrestricted when they create incentives to bias publication decisions.
wrong.C: Copyright is a legitimate operational concern but is not the primary ethical issue raised by COPE in this context.
wrong.D: The question specifically asks about the commercial concern — the structural incentive created by reprint revenue is the ethically recognised problem.

explanation.guideline: COPE — financial relationships between publishers and commercial entities should be transparent and should not influence editorial decisions
explanation.trial: Lundh A et al. Industry sponsorship and research outcome (Cochrane Review 2017) — industry-sponsored trials are 4 times more likely to report positive results.
explanation.pearl: Impact factor inflation through reprint-driven acceptance of industry-sponsored trials is a recognised form of impact factor manipulation — it biases both the IF and the literature.

questions.stem: A researcher posts a manuscript on medRxiv before journal submission. Three months later, the paper is accepted by BJS. A systematic review team wishes to cite the medRxiv preprint in their Methods section as the source of the dataset. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Cite the medRxiv preprint — it is the primary source and was available first
options.B: The published BJS version should be cited; the preprint should not be cited unless the published version is behind a paywall
options.C: Cite the published BJS version; if the preprint contained data not in the final publication, the preprint may be cited alongside the published version with explicit notation that it is a preprint
options.D: Preprints cannot be cited in systematic reviews — only peer-reviewed publications are eligible for inclusion

explanation.correct: Once a peer-reviewed version is available, the published article is the definitive scientific record and should be the primary citation. Preprints should not be cited in place of available peer-reviewed publications. However, if the preprint contained data elements that were removed or changed in the published version (which is common — peer review often changes analyses), citing the preprint alongside the published version with clear labelling ('preprint, not peer reviewed') may be appropriate with explicit explanation. For systematic reviews, PRISMA 2020 allows inclusion of preprints as study evidence but requires explicit notation of their preprint status.

wrong.A: Priority of posting does not make the preprint the more authoritative source — peer review improves accuracy and reliability.
wrong.B: Open access status of the published version is not the criterion for whether to cite the preprint — always cite the peer-reviewed version when available.
wrong.D: PRISMA 2020 and most systematic review guidelines now explicitly allow preprint inclusion with appropriate labelling — blanket exclusion of preprints can bias systematic reviews.

explanation.guideline: PRISMA 2020 — preprints may be included in systematic reviews with clear notation; ICMJE — preprints should be clearly labelled and distinguished from peer-reviewed publications
explanation.trial: Fraser N et al. Preprinting the COVID-19 pandemic. eLife 2021 — analysis of preprint use in COVID-19 research and its strengths and risks.
explanation.pearl: When citing a preprint: always add '(preprint, not peer reviewed)' in the citation text; check at submission/publication whether the preprint has since been published and update citations accordingly.

questions.stem: An author discovers after publication that Table 3 of their paper contains a systematic data entry error — the complication rates in the two groups were accidentally transposed. The error does not change the direction of the primary outcome OR its statistical significance, but the percentages are incorrect. What is the appropriate post-publication action?

options.A: No action needed — the primary outcome is unchanged
options.B: Submit a Corrigendum (author correction) to the journal specifying the error, the correct values, and confirming the conclusions remain valid
options.C: Request retraction of the paper and resubmission with the corrected table
options.D: Post a correction notice on ResearchGate and notify the corresponding author's institution

explanation.correct: A Corrigendum is the appropriate mechanism for an author error that, when corrected, does not invalidate the paper's conclusions. The journal publishes a formal Corrigendum notice linked to the original article, providing the correct table values and confirming that conclusions remain valid. This is the COPE-recommended approach: transparent correction of the scientific record without the disproportionate response of retraction. The Corrigendum is indexed in PubMed alongside the original article, ensuring readers who find the original encounter the correction.

wrong.A: Even when conclusions are unchanged, incorrect data in a published paper must be corrected — readers citing Table 3 would perpetuate the error.
wrong.C: Retraction followed by resubmission would be disproportionate for an error that does not invalidate conclusions — retraction is reserved for errors that make conclusions unreliable or for misconduct.
wrong.D: Informal notices on preprint servers or social media do not constitute official scientific record corrections — they are invisible to most readers and not indexed in databases.

explanation.guideline: COPE Retraction and Correction Guidelines — Corrigendum for author errors that do not invalidate conclusions; Erratum for journal production errors; Retraction for errors that invalidate conclusions
explanation.trial: Errami M, Garner H. A tale of two citations. Nature 2008 — analysis of the frequency of citation of retracted and corrected papers.
explanation.pearl: Distinction: **Erratum** = journal production error (typesetting, layout); **Corrigendum** = author error in submitted content. Both correct the record without retraction when conclusions remain valid.

questions.stem: A journal editor receives a complaint that two figures in a published surgical outcomes paper appear to have been digitally manipulated — the Western blot bands appear to have been cut and pasted from different experiments. An investigation is opened. Before the investigation is concluded, what is the appropriate editorial action?

options.A: Retract the paper immediately pending investigation results
options.B: Publish an Expression of Concern, notifying readers that concerns have been raised and an investigation is underway
options.C: Take no action until the investigation is complete — publishing premature notices unfairly damages the authors' reputation
options.D: Contact the authors directly and, if they deny wrongdoing, take no further action

explanation.correct: An Expression of Concern (EoC) is the appropriate intermediate step when serious integrity concerns have been raised but the investigation is not yet complete. The EoC notifies readers that the article is under investigation without prejudging the outcome — it protects readers from uncritically relying on potentially flawed data while respecting the presumption of innocence during ongoing investigation. COPE guidelines recommend EoCs precisely for this situation: where concerns are credible, an investigation has been opened, but facts have not yet been established. Retraction before investigation completion would be premature and potentially defamatory.

wrong.A: Immediate retraction without completed investigation is premature — investigations may find the concern unfounded, and premature retraction is itself a harm to the authors.
wrong.C: Complete inaction when credible concerns have been raised fails the journal's responsibility to the scientific community — readers deserve to know that an article is under scrutiny.
wrong.D: Authors denying wrongdoing does not resolve the investigation — independent assessment of the images is required, not self-reporting by potentially implicated authors.

explanation.guideline: COPE Guidelines 2022 — 'An expression of concern should be considered when an investigation is underway but a definitive finding cannot yet be reached; it alerts readers to potential problems'
explanation.trial: Budd JM et al. Retracted publications in biomedicine: cause for concern. Bull Med Libr Assoc 1998 — foundational analysis of retraction mechanisms.
explanation.pearl: EoC → retraction or exoneration: the investigation determines the final action. If images are confirmed manipulated → retraction. If manipulation is not substantiated → EoC withdrawn and article remains with editorial note.

questions.stem: A surgical trainee self-archives their published EJSO article on ResearchGate, uploading the final published PDF (Version of Record) without checking EJSO's self-archiving policy. EJSO is an Elsevier hybrid journal. Which statement is most likely correct about the copyright status of this action?

options.A: Self-archiving on ResearchGate is always permitted for any published article
options.B: Self-archiving the final published PDF on ResearchGate without checking the publisher's policy may violate Elsevier's copyright if the article was not published as Open Access
options.C: ResearchGate is an institutional repository and self-archiving is always permitted regardless of copyright
options.D: Self-archiving is only prohibited for articles older than 12 months

explanation.correct: For subscription journal articles (not published as OA), the publisher holds copyright over the Version of Record (VoR — the final formatted PDF). Self-archiving the VoR on ResearchGate without the publisher's permission may violate the copyright agreement signed at submission. Elsevier's policy (for hybrid journals without OA payment) typically permits: self-archiving of the Accepted Manuscript (AM — the peer-reviewed version before journal formatting) in repositories after a 12-month embargo; but NOT free distribution of the VoR except in authorised institutional repositories. ResearchGate is not an institutional repository in the COPE sense — it is a commercial social network. Authors should check journal self-archiving policies via SHERPA/RoMEO before uploading any version.

wrong.A: Self-archiving rights depend on the copyright agreement and publisher policy — not all articles may be self-archived in all versions on all platforms.
wrong.C: ResearchGate is a commercial social network platform, not an institutional repository — it has different permissions than a university repository.
wrong.D: The 12-month embargo applies to the Accepted Manuscript in repositories, not to the Version of Record, which may have different and stricter permissions.

explanation.guideline: ICMJE — authors must understand the copyright agreements they sign; SHERPA/RoMEO database provides self-archiving policies for all major journals
explanation.trial: Tennant JP et al. The academic, economic and societal impacts of Open Access. F1000Research 2016.
explanation.pearl: Check self-archiving rights at SHERPA/RoMEO (v2.jisc.ac.uk) before uploading any manuscript version to any platform. Three manuscript versions: (1) Preprint (pre-peer review) — most often freely shareable; (2) Accepted Manuscript (post-review, pre-formatting) — embargo period typically 12 months; (3) Version of Record (final formatted PDF) — usually restricted to publisher site unless OA.

questions.stem: A researcher's ISCIII-funded study is accepted by a hybrid journal (subscription + OA option). The APC for Gold OA is €3,200. The researcher's institution has a Transformative Agreement with the publisher. What is the most appropriate course of action?

options.A: Pay the €3,200 APC from the research grant
options.B: Publish without OA — ISCIII funding does not mandate OA
options.C: Check whether the Transformative Agreement covers the APC, enabling compliant Gold OA at no additional cost; if not covered, explore Green OA via immediate deposit in a compliant repository
options.D: Request a waiver based on geographic location — Spain qualifies for automatic APC waivers

explanation.correct: Transformative Agreements (TAs) are contracts between institutions/consortia and publishers that bundle subscription access and APC costs, allowing researchers at signatory institutions to publish OA in hybrid journals without paying individual APCs. If the researcher's institution has a TA with the hybrid journal's publisher, the OA publication may be covered — this should be checked first via the institution's library. If the TA does not cover this journal, the ISCIII mandate can be satisfied via Green OA: immediate deposit of the Accepted Manuscript in a compliant repository (DIGITAL.CSIC, PubMed Central) under CC-BY licence with no embargo. This eliminates the APC cost while achieving Plan S compliance.

wrong.A: Before paying an APC, always check whether a Transformative Agreement covers it — paying unnecessarily wastes research funds.
wrong.B: ISCIII joined cOAlition S in 2022, making OA mandatory for all funded research — publishing without OA compliance violates the funding terms.
wrong.D: Spain is not classified as a low-income country qualifying for automatic geographic waivers — these apply to World Bank low-income countries, not Spain.

explanation.guideline: Plan S / cOAlition S — ISCIII-funded research must be OA with immediate CC-BY licence; Transformative Agreements and Green OA are compliant routes; Journal Checker Tool (journalcheckertool.org) verifies compliance
explanation.trial: Laakso M et al. The development of open access journal publishing from 1993 to 2009. PLOS ONE 2011.
explanation.pearl: Use the Journal Checker Tool (journalcheckertool.org) — input your funder (ISCIII) and journal — to get a specific compliance determination and the lowest-cost compliant route.

questions.stem: A researcher's H-index is reported as 18 on Google Scholar, 14 on Scopus, and 12 on Web of Science for the same person and publication list. Which statement best explains this discrepancy?

options.A: The discrepancy indicates data errors in one or more databases
options.B: Different databases index different publication types and citation sources — Google Scholar is most inclusive; Web of Science most restrictive
options.C: The H-index should only be reported from Web of Science as it is the most rigorous database
options.D: H-index differences between databases exceed 2 points only for researchers with fewer than 5 publications

explanation.correct: The H-index varies across databases because each indexes different sources: Google Scholar includes peer-reviewed journals, books, book chapters, conference proceedings, dissertations, patents, and grey literature — and is freely accessible; Scopus (Elsevier) covers journals, conference proceedings, and book series with a quality filter; Web of Science (Clarivate) is the most restrictive, primarily covering high-quality journals with minimal conference proceedings and no books. A researcher with publications in conference proceedings or book chapters will have a higher Google Scholar H-index than Web of Science H-index. When reporting H-index in academic applications: always specify the database and date of access.

wrong.A: The discrepancy is systematic and expected — it reflects different coverage, not data errors.
wrong.C: Web of Science is rigorous but restrictive — for fields with significant conference or book publication, it underestimates impact. No single database is universally superior for all fields.
wrong.D: Database discrepancies can be substantial for researchers with many publications — the gap between Google Scholar and Web of Science H-index often exceeds 5–10 points for prolific researchers.

explanation.guideline: No single guideline mandates one database — the European Research Council recommends multiple metrics from multiple sources
explanation.trial: Harzing AW, van der Wal R. Google Scholar as a new source for citation analysis. Ethics in Science and Environmental Politics 2008.
explanation.pearl: H-index reporting convention: always state '(Google Scholar, accessed [date])' or '(Scopus, accessed [date])'. For grant applications, some funders specify which database to use — check before applying.

questions.stem: A junior surgeon receives an unsolicited email inviting them to submit a manuscript to the 'International Journal of Surgery and Surgical Techniques', promising peer review within 5 days and publication within 2 weeks, with a €600 APC. The journal claims an impact factor of 4.2 but is not listed on PubMed or Scopus. What is the most likely explanation?

options.A: A newly founded journal with a rapid publication model — rapid review is a feature, not a defect
options.B: A predatory journal — multiple red flags are present including unsolicited invitation, implausibly rapid review, APC without legitimate indexing, and fabricated impact factor
options.C: An Open Access journal with lower standards than subscription journals
options.D: A legitimate journal in its first year of operation that has not yet been indexed

explanation.correct: Multiple red flags are present simultaneously: (1) unsolicited invitation email — legitimate journals do not recruit authors via mass email; (2) peer review in 5 days — genuine peer review from subject experts takes 3–6 weeks; (3) publication within 2 weeks — incompatible with legitimate revision and production processes; (4) claimed IF of 4.2 without PubMed or Scopus indexing — the impact factor is calculated by Clarivate only for journals indexed in Web of Science, not all of which are in PubMed; however, a journal claiming IF 4.2 without any major database indexing has a fabricated or unofficial IF; (5) APC of €600 for non-indexed publication. Any two of these flags together should trigger verification — five flags together confirm predatory journal.

wrong.A: Rapid publication is achievable with legitimate models (PLOS ONE uses structured review, decisions in ~4–6 weeks) but 5-day peer review does not allow genuine expert assessment.
wrong.C: Open access journals can be fully legitimate (PLOS Medicine, BMC Surgery) — predatory journals exploit the OA model but are not defined by their access model.
wrong.D: New journals legitimately in their first year would have transparent editorial boards, verifiable review processes, and would not claim IF — and would not use unsolicited mass recruitment.

explanation.guideline: COPE — predatory journals violate publication ethics by misrepresenting peer review processes and claiming false credentials
explanation.trial: Bohannon J. Who's Afraid of Peer Review? Science 2013 — 157 predatory journals accepted a fatally flawed paper.
explanation.pearl: Verification checklist for any journal: (1) Is it indexed in PubMed/MEDLINE? (2) Is it indexed in Scopus? (3) Is the publisher a COPE member? (4) Is the IF verifiable in Clarivate Journal Citation Reports? (5) Does the journal have a verifiable editorial board? Fail any two → investigate further before submitting.

questions.stem: A manuscript is submitted that closely resembles a previously published paper from the same group, with different patient selection dates but similar text and all the same baseline characteristics table. An editor uses iThenticate and finds 48% text similarity. Which COPE flowchart should guide the editor's response?

options.A: Suspected plagiarism in a submitted manuscript
options.B: Suspected duplicate publication in a submitted manuscript
options.C: Suspected data fabrication in a submitted manuscript
options.D: Conflict of interest undisclosed by an author

explanation.correct: The scenario describes suspected duplicate publication (also termed redundant publication) — submission of a manuscript that substantially overlaps with a previously published paper by the same authors, without appropriate disclosure of the prior publication. This differs from plagiarism (copying text from OTHER authors' work). The identical baseline characteristics table is particularly suspicious — if the patient cohorts genuinely differ in dates, baseline characteristics would be expected to differ. COPE provides a specific flowchart for 'Suspected duplicate publication in a submitted manuscript' which guides editors through the steps of: confirming the overlap; contacting authors for explanation; if confirmed, rejecting the submission and potentially notifying the original journal.

wrong.A: Plagiarism involves using others' text without attribution — here, the suspected issue is self-duplication of the authors' own prior work, not copying from other authors.
wrong.C: Data fabrication (inventing data) is not specifically suggested — the concern here is duplicate reporting of the same real data.
wrong.D: Conflict of interest is not the primary identified concern — the overlap between submissions is the central issue.

explanation.guideline: COPE flowchart C14 — Suspected duplicate publication in a submitted manuscript; ICMJE — duplicate publication is a violation of publication ethics
explanation.trial: Von Elm E et al. Different patterns of duplicate publication: an analysis of articles used in systematic reviews. JAMA 2004.
explanation.pearl: iThenticate/Crossref Similarity Check generates a similarity score — percentage alone does not determine plagiarism or duplication (high similarity may reflect quoted methods sections or common field terminology). Context and source of similar text matter more than the percentage.

questions.stem: A clinical oncology journal reports an impact factor of 28.4. Examination of the cited references reveals that 35% of all citations in 2022–2023 were to papers published in the same journal. Which metric manipulation strategy is most likely being employed?

options.A: Publication bias — preferential publication of positive results inflating the IF
options.B: Journal self-citation — the journal preferentially publishes or cites its own papers to inflate the IF calculation
options.C: Citation cartels — reciprocal citation agreements between multiple journals
options.D: Coercive citation — reviewers requesting authors add unjustified references

explanation.correct: Journal self-citation inflates the IF because the impact factor numerator counts all citations (including self-citations) to papers published in the journal in the prior 2 years, from the same journal. A journal that publishes many review articles citing its own research papers, or that editorialises by citing its own prior editorials, can significantly inflate its calculated IF. Clarivate monitors self-citation rates — journals with excessive self-citation (>20–25% of citations being self-citations) may be suppressed from the IF calculation or flagged. The IF of a journal with 35% self-citation is substantially inflated and not comparable to journals with normal self-citation rates (~10%).

wrong.A: Publication bias inflates effect sizes in the literature but does not directly inflate a journal's IF through the self-citation mechanism.
wrong.C: Citation cartels involve reciprocal agreements between different journals — the scenario describes within-journal self-citation, not inter-journal arrangements.
wrong.D: Coercive citation involves reviewers requesting authors add unjustified references — not systematically reflected in a 35% self-citation rate in the journal's own published papers.

explanation.guideline: Clarivate Journal Citation Reports — journals with excessive self-citation rates may be suppressed from IF calculations
explanation.trial: Falagas ME et al. The impact factor and its use in scholarly publishing and the assessment of research. Eur J Clin Pharmacol 2010.
explanation.pearl: When evaluating journal quality, look beyond the raw IF: check the self-citation rate (available in Clarivate's Journal Citation Reports); check CiteScore (which uses a 4-year window — less susceptible to gaming); and check whether the journal is indexed in multiple databases.

questions.stem: A researcher wishes to assess the real-world translation of their CRS+HIPEC outcomes paper published 6 months ago. The paper has 8 citations in Scopus but an Altmetric score of 142. Which interpretation is most accurate?

options.A: An Altmetric score of 142 indicates the paper will eventually accumulate many citations — it is a leading indicator of long-term citation impact
options.B: The high Altmetric score indicates strong non-academic engagement (social media, news, policy) that is not yet reflected in formal citation counts; Altmetrics and citations measure different dimensions of impact
options.C: The Altmetric score is inflated — 8 citations in 6 months indicates the paper is not widely valued by the scientific community
options.D: Altmetric scores above 100 confirm that the paper has had clinical practice change impact

explanation.correct: Altmetric scores and citation counts measure fundamentally different phenomena. Altmetric score is a composite of social media mentions (Twitter/X, Facebook, Reddit), news coverage, blog posts, policy document citations, and Wikipedia references — primarily reflecting public engagement and media interest rather than scientific peer endorsement. Citation counts measure how much the scientific community has engaged with the work in subsequent publications — a process that takes 2–5 years to accumulate. A high Altmetric score with low citation count after 6 months is common and does not indicate a contradiction — the paper has resonated with public or clinical audiences but has not yet been cited by subsequent researchers. Some papers with very high Altmetric scores (e.g., COVID-19 papers) accumulate citations rapidly; others remain primarily in the public sphere.

wrong.A: Altmetric scores are not reliable leading indicators of long-term citation impact — the correlation between Altmetric score and 5-year citations is moderate at best and field-dependent.
wrong.C: 8 citations in 6 months is actually quite high for a surgical oncology paper — citation accumulation takes years. The Altmetric score does not negate this.
wrong.D: No Altmetric score threshold reliably indicates clinical practice change — clinical uptake is measured by guideline inclusion, not social media engagement.

explanation.guideline: No formal guideline — Altmetric scores are complementary to, not replacements for, citation-based impact metrics
explanation.trial: Haustein S et al. Tweeting biomedicine: an analysis of tweets and citations in the biomedical literature. J Am Soc Inf Sci Technol 2014.
explanation.pearl: Report impact using multiple metrics: citation count (Scopus/WoS) for scientific impact; Altmetric score for public engagement; downloads for early dissemination interest. No single metric captures the full picture.

questions.stem: A researcher completes a systematic review and meta-analysis of HIPEC approaches for colorectal peritoneal metastases. A colleague suggests posting the analysis on medRxiv before journal submission. The corresponding author is concerned about whether journal submission after medRxiv posting will be permitted. What is the ICMJE position on this?

options.A: ICMJE member journals do not permit submission of manuscripts previously posted as preprints
options.B: ICMJE member journals permit submission of manuscripts previously posted as preprints, provided the journal is informed of the preprint at submission
options.C: Preprint posting is permitted only for randomised trial reports, not systematic reviews
options.D: Preprint posting constitutes prior publication and violates ICMJE Ingelfinger rule

explanation.correct: ICMJE updated its policy in 2016 to explicitly state that preprint posting is not considered prior publication under the ICMJE definition. Authors may post preprints on recognised servers (medRxiv, bioRxiv, SSRN, Research Square) before or during journal submission, provided: (1) the preprint is disclosed at journal submission; (2) the preprint is updated to link to the published version after acceptance; (3) the journal is notified. The vast majority of ICMJE member journals (including BJS, ASO, EJSO, Lancet, NEJM) now explicitly permit preprint posting. However, some journals have specific policies or restrictions — always check the target journal's Instructions to Authors.

wrong.A: This was true before the 2016 ICMJE policy update — it is now outdated.
wrong.C: ICMJE's preprint policy applies to all manuscript types, not only randomised trials.
wrong.D: The Ingelfinger rule (named after former NEJM editor Franz Ingelfinger) originally prohibited submission of manuscripts that had been reported in any media. The rule has been significantly liberalised — preprints are now explicitly excluded from its prohibition.

explanation.guideline: ICMJE Recommendations 2024 — 'Posting of manuscripts to preprint servers is not considered prior publication; authors should inform editors at the time of submission if the paper has been posted as a preprint'
explanation.trial: Desjardins-Proulx P et al. The case for open preprints in biology. PLOS Biology 2013.
explanation.pearl: Preprint posting advantages: rapid dissemination (months before publication); priority claim (timestamped); community feedback before formal peer review; improved manuscript quality at submission. Check target journal policy at SHERPA/RoMEO or journal website before posting.

questions.stem: A group wishes to publish a surgical technique paper describing a modified approach to peritoneal port placement for PIPAC. The technique has been used in 12 patients with no comparison group. Which journal type and article format is most appropriate?

options.A: Full Original Article in EJSO — surgical innovation warrants full manuscript format
options.B: Technical Note or Surgical Innovation report in a specialty journal (EJSO, Surgical Endoscopy) — appropriate format for novel technique description without comparative efficacy data
options.C: Case series in Annals of Surgery — higher impact factor maximises technique visibility
options.D: Letter to the Editor in BJS — technique papers are always published as correspondence

explanation.correct: A Technical Note or Surgical Innovation report is the appropriate format for description of a novel technique without comparative efficacy data. This format allows complete technique description (often with intraoperative images or video supplementary), case series feasibility data (safety, technical success rate), and preliminary outcome data — without claiming comparative efficacy that 12 cases without controls cannot support. EJSO publishes a specific 'Surgical Technology' section; Surgical Endoscopy publishes 'Technical Innovation' articles; Colorectal Disease publishes 'Technique' papers. These formats have defined word limits (~800–1500 words) and figure allowances specifically calibrated for technique description.

wrong.A: Original Article format implies comparative efficacy data and a defined primary outcome with hypothesis testing — inappropriate for 12-case technical feasibility data.
wrong.C: Annals of Surgery accepts surgical technique papers but the journal's audience and acceptance criteria are primarily calibrated for large comparative studies — a 12-case technical note would be a very poor scope match.
wrong.D: Letters to the Editor are for correspondence about published papers — not for primary technical descriptions with their own original data.

explanation.guideline: Journal-specific — check Instructions to Authors for Technical Note format specifications (typically 800–1500 words, 3–5 figures, 10–15 references)
explanation.trial: Demartines N et al. SCARE criteria for surgical case reports — distinguishing case reports and surgical techniques.
explanation.pearl: Technical Note checklist: (1) describe each procedural step clearly; (2) include intraoperative images at key steps; (3) specify learning curve and surgeon volume prerequisites; (4) report safety data (30-day morbidity at minimum); (5) discuss how the technique improves on prior approaches. A video supplement significantly increases engagement and citation.

questions.stem: A surgical resident uses an AI writing tool (GPT-4) to generate a first draft of the Introduction and Discussion sections of their manuscript. The final manuscript has been reviewed and critically revised by all five authors. Which statement about AI disclosure in this context is correct according to current ICMJE guidance?

options.A: AI tools may be listed as co-authors if they made substantial contributions to writing
options.B: AI tool use must be disclosed in the manuscript Methods or Acknowledgements; AI tools cannot be listed as authors; all authors are accountable for AI-generated content
options.C: Disclosure is optional if human authors have substantially revised the AI-generated text
options.D: AI tool disclosure is only required if the tool was used for data analysis, not writing

explanation.correct: ICMJE (2023 update) and virtually all major journals have issued clear statements: (1) AI tools cannot be listed as authors — they cannot meet ICMJE criterion 4 (accountability); (2) use of AI in manuscript preparation must be disclosed in the manuscript (Methods for data analysis; Acknowledgements or Cover Letter for writing assistance); (3) the corresponding author takes full responsibility for the accuracy and integrity of AI-generated content. The disclosure is not optional — failure to declare AI tool use is increasingly treated as an ethical violation equivalent to other non-disclosure failures.

wrong.A: No journal accepts AI tools as authors — ICMJE, Nature, Science, and all major medical journals explicitly prohibit this.
wrong.C: Disclosure obligation exists regardless of the extent of human revision — transparency about methodology is the principle.
wrong.D: Disclosure applies to any AI use in manuscript preparation — writing, editing, image generation, data analysis, or statistical interpretation.

explanation.guideline: ICMJE 2023 update on AI in academic publishing — mandatory disclosure; non-authorship of AI tools; corresponding author accountability
explanation.trial: Thorp HH. ChatGPT is fun, but not an author. Science 2023 — editorial establishing non-authorship of AI tools.
explanation.pearl: Recommended AI disclosure template: 'The authors used [Tool Name vX] (Company) to generate an initial draft of the Introduction and Discussion sections. This AI-generated content was subsequently reviewed, substantially revised, and verified for accuracy by all authors, who take full responsibility for the integrity of the final manuscript.'

questions.stem: A journal editor contacts a researcher to inform them that a reviewer has flagged their published paper as possibly containing fabricated data — the reviewer noticed identical standard deviations across multiple independent samples, which is statistically implausible. The researcher denies any fabrication. According to COPE guidelines, what is the appropriate next step for the editor?

options.A: Accept the authors' denial and close the investigation
options.B: Immediately retract the paper based on the reviewer's assessment
options.C: Notify the authors' institution and request that the institution conduct a formal investigation; publish an Expression of Concern in the interim
options.D: Request that a statistician reanalyse the data to determine if fabrication occurred, without institutional notification

explanation.correct: COPE guidelines for suspected data fabrication require: (1) the editor cannot adjudicate fabrication independently — institutional investigation by the authors' employing institution is the appropriate mechanism, as institutions have the authority to access raw data, lab notebooks, and conduct formal misconduct proceedings; (2) an Expression of Concern should be published to alert readers while the investigation is underway; (3) the editor should not simply accept denials, nor independently retract based on statistical analysis alone. Authors' institutions have formal research integrity procedures and investigators with appropriate authority. The editor's role is to refer to institutional processes and protect the scientific record via EoC.

wrong.A: Authors' denial is insufficient to close an investigation into potentially serious misconduct — independent investigation is required.
wrong.B: Immediate retraction without completed investigation could be defamatory and is contrary to due process — retraction is the conclusion of an investigation, not its substitute.
wrong.D: Statistical reanalysis by an independent statistician is a useful supplementary step but does not substitute for institutional investigation; and individual statistical analysis cannot prove or disprove fabrication without access to raw data.

explanation.guideline: COPE Guidelines — Suspected research misconduct: editors should notify the institution; publish EoC if appropriate; cooperate with institutional investigation; take definitive action (retraction or exoneration notice) after investigation concludes
explanation.trial: Fanelli D. How many scientists fabricate and falsify research? A systematic review and meta-analysis. PLOS ONE 2009 — ~2% of scientists admit to data fabrication.
explanation.pearl: Statistically implausible data patterns are an established detection tool for fabrication: too-round numbers, identical standard deviations across samples, normally distributed data with suspiciously symmetric tails, and digit frequency analysis (Benford's law for first-digit frequency) are all used by data forensic investigators.

questions.stem: An open peer review journal (where reviewer identities are disclosed) publishes a review in which the reviewer, identified by name, makes highly critical comments suggesting the paper contains fundamental flaws. The paper is accepted with minor revision and published. The reviewer subsequently contacts the editor claiming professional retaliation — she was rejected for a grant where the corresponding author was on the review panel. What is the primary ethical concern with open peer review in this scenario?

options.A: Open peer review eliminates reviewer bias — retaliation claims are unfounded
options.B: Open peer review systems create retaliation risk for critical reviewers, particularly junior researchers and those in small competitive fields — this is a documented limitation requiring careful editorial management
options.C: The reviewer should not have disclosed their identity — open peer review is always optional
options.D: The grant rejection constitutes proof of retaliation and warrants investigation

explanation.correct: Reviewer retaliation risk is one of the most cited documented limitations of open peer review. When reviewer identities are disclosed, reviewers in small, competitive fields face potential professional consequences for providing critical but honest reviews — particularly junior researchers reviewing senior colleagues' work. Studies on open peer review implementation (BMJ, PLOS Medicine) have shown that reviewers are more likely to decline review invitations or moderate their criticism when their identity will be disclosed. This creates a paradox: open peer review improves transparency and accountability, but reduces reviewer willingness to provide critical assessment. Most journals that implement open peer review offer it as opt-in for reviewers precisely to mitigate this concern.

wrong.A: Open peer review does not eliminate reviewer bias — it changes the type of bias, potentially reducing prestige bias but increasing retaliation concern.
wrong.C: In journals that implement open peer review, it may be the journal's policy (not optional) — the reviewer accepted the review under that policy.
wrong.D: Grant rejection after a critical review is suspicious timing but is not proof of retaliation — correlation does not establish causation; the grant decision may have had independent merit-based reasons.

explanation.guideline: COPE — editors should have mechanisms to protect reviewers from retaliation; retaliation concerns should be investigated; reviewer identities should be protected if confidentiality is requested
explanation.trial: Van Rooyen S et al. Effect of open peer review on quality of reviews and on reviewers' recommendations: a randomised trial. BMJ 1999 — foundational RCT on open vs blinded peer review.
explanation.pearl: Open peer review trade-off: increased transparency and accountability vs reduced reviewer willingness to provide critical assessment. Journals that implement open peer review should offer opt-in/opt-out for reviewers and have explicit anti-retaliation policies.
